Option B is incorrect because while Ghon preserves some relationships, the en masse approach is superior for the entire GI tract. Option C is incorrect because in-situ dissection is too cramped for detailed mesenteric vascular tracing. Option E is incorrect as it is a synonym for the Virchow method.

options.E: Organ-by-organ method

questions.question: In the Letulle technique, what is the most critical step required to mobilize the thoracic organs while keeping them attached to the abdominal viscera?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because to remove the thoracic and abdominal organs as a single "en masse" unit, the diaphragm must be incised circularly to allow the thoracic organs to pass into the abdominal cavity or vice versa. Option A is incorrect as this is a standard step for head/neck access but not the defining step for en masse mobilization. Option C is incorrect as this is required for all thoracic entries. Option D is incorrect as this relates to the pelvic block. Option E is incorrect as this is a step in mobilizing the retroperitoneum but not the specific step that connects the two major cavities.

options.B: Complete circular incision of the diaphragm
options.C: Removal of the sternum and ribs
options.E: Separation of the kidneys from the psoas muscle

questions.question: A forensic pathologist needs to trace the path of a swallowed foreign body that has potentially perforated the junction between the pharynx and the esophagus. Which technique is most appropriate?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the Ghon method (en bloc) typically removes the "cervical block" (tongue, larynx, esophagus) which allows for the inspection of the pharyngeal-esophageal junction without disruption. Option A is incorrect because individual removal would likely involve cutting through the area of interest. Option C is incorrect because the neck is very difficult to dissect in-situ. Option D is incorrect because Letulle is more extensive than necessary for a localized neck/thoracic issue. Option E is incorrect as it is a synonym for the Rokitansky method.

options.E: In-situ method

questions.question: A 65-year-old female dies of suspected complications from a pelvic tumor that has invaded the ureters and the rectum. To preserve the entire urinary tract from the kidneys down to the bladder along with the reproductive organs, which technique should be utilized?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the Ghon method uses the "en bloc" approach, where the urogenital system (kidneys, ureters, bladder, and pelvic organs) can be removed as a single functional block, preserving their anatomical relationships. Option A is incorrect because it would involve removing the kidneys and bladder separately, severing the ureters. Option C is incorrect because in-situ dissection of the deep pelvis is technically difficult and provides poor visualization. Option D is incorrect because while Letulle preserves these, the Ghon "urogenital block" is the specific standard for this clinical need. Option E is incorrect as it is a synonym for the Virchow method.

options.E: Individual organ removal

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of the Letulle method?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the Letulle method is defined by the "en masse" removal of all viscera as one unit. Option A is incorrect as that describes the Rokitansky method. Option B is incorrect as that describes the Ghon method. Option D is incorrect as that is only one part of the Ghon method. Option E is incorrect because the order of brain removal is not the defining characteristic of the Letulle method.

options.A: Organs are never removed from the body
options.B: Organs are removed in three separate blocks
options.C: All organs are removed as a single mass
options.D: Only the heart and lungs are removed together
options.E: The brain is removed before the thoracic organs

questions.question: During a forensic autopsy of a victim with a suspected pulmonary embolism, the pathologist wants to examine the heart and lungs together to see the embolus in the pulmonary artery. Which method is being used if these organs are removed as one unit?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because removing the heart and lungs together as a functional unit is an example of the "en bloc" technique, which is the Ghon method. Option A is incorrect because Virchow would remove the heart and lungs separately. Option C is incorrect because Rokitansky would dissect them inside the chest. Option D is incorrect because Letulle would remove them along with the abdominal organs. Option E is incorrect as it is not a standard term for this procedure.

options.E: En-situ method

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: During a forensic autopsy of a suspected case of poisoning by a cerebral depressant, which specific portion and quantity of the brain must be preserved for toxicological analysis?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the standard protocol for toxicological analysis in suspected cerebral poisoning requires half of the brain or approximately 500 grams to ensure a sufficient sample for detection. Option A is incorrect as 100 grams is insufficient for a comprehensive toxicological screen. Option C is incorrect as the brain stem alone does not provide enough tissue mass for multiple analytical tests. Option D is incorrect because while hemispheres are important, the protocol specifies a larger portion of the whole brain to include various functional areas. Option E is incorrect as the medulla is too small to meet the 500-gram requirement.

options.E: 250 grams of the medulla oblongata

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: Which chemical is added to a blood sample to prevent the neo-formation of alcohol by microbes during transit?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because Sodium fluoride acts as an enzyme inhibitor that prevents microbial fermentation and the subsequent production of "post-mortem" alcohol in the sample. Option A is incorrect as it is an anticoagulant, not a preservative against fermentation. Option B is incorrect as it is primarily an anticoagulant used to prevent blood clotting. Option D is incorrect as it is an anticoagulant used for clinical blood tests rather than forensic preservation. Option E is incorrect as it is a chelating agent used for blood counts.

options.E: EDTA

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: A 22-year-old known drug addict is found dead with a fresh needle mark in the antecubital fossa. To confirm the presence of the injected substance, how should the skin at the site be collected?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the protocol for injected poisons requires the excision of the suspected injection site along with a control sample from a similar, uninvolved area (usually the opposite limb) to differentiate between the injected drug and systemic levels. Option A is incorrect as a small punch biopsy may miss the concentrated drug deposit in the surrounding tissue. Option C is incorrect as swabbing only collects surface contaminants and spirit may interfere with the chemical analysis. Option D is incorrect as the skin and the needle track are both necessary for analysis. Option E is incorrect as the skin entry point is vital for the forensic evidence of the injection.

options.A: A 1 cm circular punch biopsy of the puncture mark.
options.B: Excision of the puncture site along with a control sample from the opposite limb.
options.C: Swabbing the surface of the puncture mark with rectified spirit.
options.D: Deep incision to collect only the underlying subcutaneous fat.
options.E: Excision of the vein only, excluding the overlying skin.

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: When collecting the stomach for toxicological analysis, what is the standard procedure regarding its contents?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because the entire stomach and its total contents must be preserved to allow the toxicologist to calculate the total amount of unabsorbed poison present at the time of death. Option A is incorrect as the stomach wall may contain absorbed or adherent poison that is vital for detection. Option B is incorrect as washing the stomach removes the very evidence (poison) being sought. Option C is incorrect as the whole organ is required, not just a small portion. Option D is incorrect as the entire organ, including the fundus and body, is necessary for a complete analysis.

options.A: Empty the contents into a jar and discard the stomach wall.
options.B: Wash the stomach with water and preserve only the tissue.
options.C: Preserve only 100 grams of the stomach wall after emptying.
options.D: Preserve the contents and only the pyloric end of the stomach.
options.E: Preserve the entire stomach along with all its contents.

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: A pathologist suspects that a victim died of chronic liver disease rather than acute poisoning. Which preservative should be used for the liver tissue intended for microscopic examination?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because 10% Formalin is the standard fixative for histopathological (microscopic) examination as it preserves cellular architecture and prevents autolysis. Option A is incorrect as saturated NaCl is a preservative for toxicological analysis, not histopathology. Option B is incorrect as rectified spirit hardens tissues and is generally avoided for routine histopathology. Option D is incorrect as it is not a standard tissue fixative. Option E is incorrect as it is a preservative for blood or urine in toxicology.

options.E: Sodium Fluoride

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: In a case where a narcotic poison is suspected but the stomach is empty, the medical officer decides to collect bile. What is the minimum recommended volume of bile to be preserved?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the standard forensic protocol recommends preserving the entire quantity of bile available, or at least 30 ml, as many poisons (like narcotics and heavy metals) are concentrated and excreted in the bile. Option B is incorrect as 5 ml is too small for a full toxicological screen. Option C is incorrect as 10 ml is often insufficient for multiple confirmatory tests. Option D is incorrect as the gallbladder rarely contains 100 ml of bile. Option E is incorrect as this volume exceeds the normal capacity of the gallbladder.

options.E: 250 ml

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: During an autopsy, a distinct garlic-like odor is noted upon opening the abdomen. Which preservative must be avoided for the viscera in this specific case?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because in suspected Phosphorus poisoning (indicated by the garlic odor), rectified spirit must be avoided as it can dissolve the phosphorus and destroy its characteristic luminosity, which is a diagnostic feature. Option A is incorrect as saturated NaCl is the preferred preservative for phosphorus. Option C is incorrect as formalin is used for histopathology, not toxicology. Option D is incorrect as it is an anticoagulant. Option E is incorrect as it is a preservative for urine, not viscera.

options.E: Thymol

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: In a case of suspected death due to the systemic absorption of an insecticide through the skin, which portion of the lungs should be preserved for analysis?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because the standard protocol for toxicological analysis requires approximately 500 grams of lung tissue (usually half of each lung) to ensure adequate sampling for absorbed toxins. Option A is incorrect as the apex alone is insufficient for a representative sample. Option B is incorrect as 100 grams is below the recommended quantity for a comprehensive toxicological screen. Option C is incorrect as the protocol usually specifies a weight-based sample from both sides. Option E is incorrect as lymph nodes are not the primary site for toxicological sampling of the lungs.

options.A: Only the apex of the right lung
options.D: 500 grams of lung tissue (half of each lung)
options.E: Only the hilar lymph nodes

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: An exhumation is performed on a body buried for two years to investigate suspected chronic lead poisoning. Which sample is most likely to yield positive toxicological results?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because heavy metals like lead are deposited in the bone matrix and remain detectable for years after soft tissues have decomposed. A 10 cm segment of a long bone like the femur is the standard sample. Option A is incorrect as muscle decomposes rapidly and loses its toxicological value. Option B is incorrect as brain matter liquefies and its chemical composition changes significantly during decomposition. Option C is incorrect as putrefactive fluid is less reliable than bone for chronic metal poisoning. Option E is incorrect as long bones are preferred over flat bones like ribs for these specific analyses.

options.D: 10 cm of a long bone (e.g., femur)
options.E: A section of the rib cage

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: Why is vitreous humor increasingly collected during autopsies for toxicological analysis in cases of suspected alcohol intoxication?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the vitreous humor is located in a sterile, protected compartment, making it resistant to the microbial contamination that causes post-mortem alcohol production in blood. Option A is incorrect as vitreous humor is primarily water. Option C is incorrect as the concentration is usually correlated with plasma, not necessarily higher than the liver. Option D is incorrect as blood collection from a peripheral vein is generally simpler in a fresh body. Option E is incorrect as cremation destroys all body fluids.

options.A: It is the only fluid that does not contain water.
options.B: It is protected from post-mortem putrefactive changes and fermentation.
options.C: It contains a higher concentration of alcohol than the liver.
options.D: It is easier to collect than a blood sample in a fresh body.
options.E: It remains liquid even after the body is cremated.

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: What is the primary purpose of using a "tamper-proof seal" on viscera jars before dispatching them to the Forensic Science Laboratory?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because the seal ensures that the evidence has not been altered or tampered with from the time of the autopsy until it reaches the analyst, which is crucial for court admissibility. Option A is incorrect as the lid, not the seal, prevents evaporation. Option B is incorrect as while airtightness is good, the "tamper-proof" aspect is specifically for legal security. Option C is incorrect as the label, not the seal, carries the identification. Option D is incorrect as the seal provides no structural protection against breakage.

options.A: To prevent the preservative from evaporating.
options.B: To keep the jars airtight so the smell of the poison is retained.
options.C: To identify the name of the deceased person.
options.D: To prevent the glass jars from breaking during transit.
options.E: To ensure the legal chain of custody and prevent unauthorized access.

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: When collecting hair samples for the detection of chronic arsenic poisoning, what is the mandatory requirement for the sample?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because arsenic is deposited in the keratin and the root/bulb provides information about the timing of the ingestion; pulling ensures the entire shaft and root are available for analysis. Option A is incorrect as cutting from the tip loses the most recent growth data near the root. Option C is incorrect as scalp hair is the primary sample, though other hair can be used. Option D is incorrect as the quantity must be sufficient (usually a bunch the thickness of a pencil) and must include roots. Option E is incorrect as washing may remove external contaminants that the lab needs to analyze separately.

options.A: At least 100 strands cut from the tip.
options.B: Hair must be pulled out to include the bulbs/roots.
options.C: Hair should be shaved from the pubic region only.
options.D: Only 1 gram of hair clippings is required.
options.E: Hair must be washed in alcohol before being packed.

questions.question: What is the mandatory safety feature for all electrical outlets located in the immediate vicinity of the autopsy table?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Ground Fault Circuit Interrupters (GFCI) are mandatory in wet environments like an autopsy suite to prevent fatal electric shocks if water comes into contact with electrical equipment. Option B is incorrect as surge protectors prevent damage to equipment from voltage spikes but do not provide the necessary life-safety protection against water-induced grounding. Option C is incorrect because three-phase power is typically used for heavy industrial machinery and is not a standard safety requirement for autopsy tools. Option D is incorrect as manual reset breakers protect the circuit from overcurrent but are not sensitive enough to prevent electrocution in wet conditions. Option E is incorrect because ceramic plates are insulators used in high-heat applications and do not serve as active circuit protection in a modern medical facility.

options.E: Ceramic insulation plates

questions.question: In a standard forensic facility, what is the correct sequence of rooms for a pathologist transitioning from the public corridor to the autopsy hall?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the standard "dirty-to-clean" transition requires staff to enter a locker room to change into scrubs, pass through a shower area (if required), and then enter the autopsy hall to maintain hygiene and prevent contamination. Option A is incorrect because the office should be a clean zone and not a direct thoroughfare to the shower and hall. Option C is incorrect as this represents the exit sequence, not the entry sequence. Option D is incorrect because the hall is the "dirty" zone and should be the final destination, not an intermediate step before the locker room. Option E is incorrect because the shower is positioned between the locker room and the hall, and the office should remain outside the dirty zone.

options.E: Shower -> Hall -> Office

questions.question: A forensic facility is being renovated to improve fluid management. What is the standard architectural requirement for the floor slope to ensure efficient drainage toward the gullies?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because a floor slope of 1 in 50 is the standard engineering requirement in autopsy rooms to ensure that blood, water, and body fluids flow efficiently toward the drains without pooling. Option A is incorrect as a 1 in 10 slope would be too steep, creating a slip hazard for staff. Option B is incorrect because a 1 in 20 slope is unnecessarily steep for indoor medical drainage. Option D is incorrect as a 1 in 200 slope is too shallow and would likely result in fluid stagnation on the floor. Option E is incorrect because a 1 in 500 slope is virtually flat and would not provide the necessary gravitational pull for viscous fluids like blood.

options.E: A slope of 1 in 500

questions.question: To maintain administrative efficiency while preventing cross-contamination, where should the pathologist's report-writing station be ideally located?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because a separate clean room allows the pathologist to document findings in a sterile environment while maintaining visual contact with the autopsy hall through a window, ensuring both safety and oversight. Option A is incorrect because having a writing station inside the hall exposes paperwork and computers to aerosols and biological contaminants. Option B is incorrect as the refrigeration room is a storage area with low temperatures unsuitable for administrative work. Option C is incorrect because the public waiting area must be strictly separated from the forensic work zones for privacy and security. Option E is incorrect because the specimen museum is a specialized storage area and does not provide the necessary visual link to the ongoing procedures in the hall.

options.A: Inside the autopsy hall with a glass shield
options.D: In a separate clean room with a viewing window into the hall
options.E: In the specimen storage museum

questions.question: A forensic architect is calculating the HVAC requirements for a new high-capacity autopsy suite. To ensure adequate air volume for the mandatory 12-15 air changes per hour, what is the standard recommended ceiling height for the main autopsy hall?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because a ceiling height of 4.5 meters is recommended for high-volume autopsy halls to provide sufficient air volume for effective ventilation and to accommodate overhead tracks, lighting, and specialized exhaust systems. Option A is incorrect as 2.5 meters is a standard residential height and would be insufficient for the ventilation and equipment needs of a forensic suite. Option B is incorrect because 3.0 meters is too low to allow for the proper dispersion of formalin fumes and the installation of overhead equipment. Option C is incorrect as 3.5 meters is a minimum for small rooms but inadequate for a large, high-capacity hall. Option D is incorrect because while 4.0 meters is better, 4.5 meters is the preferred standard for modern, high-volume facilities to optimize air quality.

options.E: 4.5 meters

questions.question: Which material is most appropriate for the ceiling of an autopsy room to facilitate cleaning and prevent the accumulation of biological aerosols?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because PVC-coated metal panels are non-porous, moisture-resistant, and can be easily wiped down, making them ideal for the high-humidity and aerosol-prone environment of an autopsy room. Option B is incorrect as acoustic fiber tiles are porous and would trap moisture and biological particles, leading to mold growth. Option C is incorrect because paint on concrete can peel over time due to humidity, creating crevices for bacteria. Option D is incorrect as plywood is highly porous and impossible to disinfect effectively. Option E is incorrect because plaster of Paris is fragile, absorbs moisture, and cannot withstand the rigorous cleaning protocols required.

options.E: Decorative plaster of Paris

questions.question: When designing a viewing gallery for medical students in a forensic department, how should the communication between the pathologist and the students be facilitated?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because a two-way electronic intercom allows for clear communication between the sterile viewing gallery and the dirty autopsy hall without compromising the physical and biological barrier. Option A is incorrect as open windows would allow the passage of odors and potentially infectious aerosols into the student area. Option C is incorrect because hand signals are insufficient for explaining complex medical findings and procedures. Option D is incorrect as students should remain in the clean zone to avoid the need for full PPE and to prevent accidental exposure. Option E is incorrect because ventilation ducts are designed for air movement and would not provide clear or professional communication.

options.A: Open windows for direct voice transmission
options.D: Students entering the dirty zone for Q&A
options.E: Pathologist shouting through the ventilation ducts

questions.question: A forensic facility is being upgraded to meet modern environmental standards for liquid waste. Which feature should be integrated into the autopsy table's plumbing system?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because a macerator or grinder is required to break down small organic tissues and clots into a slurry that can be safely processed by the medical waste sewage system, preventing clogs. Option A is incorrect as a simple strainer would require manual cleaning of biological waste, increasing exposure risks for staff. Option B is incorrect because biological waste must never be discharged into rainwater drains due to environmental contamination risks. Option D is incorrect as septic tanks must be located outside the building and are not suitable for direct placement under an autopsy table. Option E is incorrect because manual filtration is inefficient and poses a significant biohazard risk to the technician.

options.B: A direct connection to the public rainwater drain
options.D: A septic tank located directly under the table
options.E: A manual filtration bucket

questions.question: For the long-term preservation of large organs in a specimen museum within the forensic department, what is the primary environmental requirement?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because specimen museums require a stable room temperature and low humidity to prevent the degradation of preservatives (like formalin) and the fading of specimens, while avoiding sunlight prevents UV damage. Option A is incorrect as 4°C is the temperature for fresh body storage, not for preserved specimens in jars. Option B is incorrect because freezing would damage the cellular structure of specimens preserved in fluid. Option C is incorrect as high temperatures and humidity would promote the growth of contaminants and degrade the mounting media. Option E is incorrect because UV light from the sun causes rapid discoloration and deterioration of biological specimens.

options.D: Room temperature with low humidity and no direct sunlight
options.E: Direct sunlight for natural drying

questions.question: To allow the pathologist to record findings without using their hands or interrupting the procedure, which piece of equipment is essential in the autopsy suite?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because a foot-operated dictation system allows the pathologist to record observations in real-time while keeping their hands free for dissection, maintaining the flow of the procedure. Option A is incorrect as a chalkboard is outdated and requires the pathologist to stop and use their hands, risking contamination. Option B is incorrect because a handheld recorder cannot be operated easily with soiled gloves and requires manual activation. Option C is incorrect as touch screens are difficult to use with wet, gloved hands and are hard to disinfect. Option D is incorrect because a stenographer would be at risk of exposure and their presence in the dirty zone is generally avoided.

options.C: A touch-screen computer on the table
options.D: A stenographer sitting at the table
options.E: A foot-operated dictation system

questions.question: In the design of the organ-washing station, what is the mandatory requirement for the sink's drainage system to prevent the aerosolization of pathogens?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because deep-set sinks combined with anti-splash baffles or covers minimize the creation of droplets and aerosols during the washing of organs, protecting the staff from inhalation of pathogens. Option B is incorrect as shallow basins increase the likelihood of splashing and aerosolization. Option C is incorrect because upward-facing jets would actively create aerosols and spread contaminants throughout the room. Option D is incorrect as open troughs are a major source of odor and contamination and are not used in modern sink designs. Option E is incorrect because overflow outlets are a standard plumbing safety feature and do not directly impact aerosolization.

options.D: Open troughs running across the floor
options.E: Sinks without any overflow outlets

questions.question: During a city-wide power outage, the forensic department must ensure that the bodies do not undergo rapid decomposition. Which system must be connected to the emergency backup generator?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because maintaining the cold chain in the mortuary refrigeration units is critical to prevent the decomposition of bodies, which would destroy forensic evidence and create a biohazard. Option A is incorrect as office cooling is a comfort feature and not a forensic necessity during an emergency. Option C is incorrect because while some lighting is needed, the refrigeration of bodies takes priority over general corridor lighting. Option D is incorrect as museum lighting is purely for display and has no impact on the preservation of the specimens themselves. Option E is incorrect because breakroom appliances are non-essential during a forensic emergency.

options.E: The staff breakroom microwave

questions.question: What is the estimated risk of seroconversion for a non-immunized prosector following a single percutaneous needle-stick injury involving a Hepatitis B (HBeAg positive) infected body?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because the risk of seroconversion after a percutaneous injury with HBeAg-positive blood is approximately 30%, making it one of the most significant biological hazards in the mortuary. Option A is incorrect as 0.3% is the estimated risk for HIV seroconversion. Option B is incorrect as 3.0% is the approximate risk for Hepatitis C. Option C is incorrect as it underestimates the high infectivity of the Hepatitis B virus in a susceptible host. Option E is incorrect as 60% is an overestimation of the risk for a single event.

questions.question: A forensic pathologist develops a firm, painless, warty papule on the dorsal aspect of the hand three months after performing an autopsy on a patient with cavitary lung lesions. What is the most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because Verruca necrogenica (prosector’s wart) is a localized form of cutaneous tuberculosis caused by accidental inoculation of Mycobacterium tuberculosis into the skin of a previously sensitized individual. Option B is incorrect as an anthrax eschar is typically a painless ulcer with a black necrotic center and significant surrounding edema. Option C is incorrect as Orf is a viral skin disease contracted from sheep/goats, presenting as a weeping nodule. Option D is incorrect as a syphilitic chancre is an indurated ulcer, not a chronic warty papule. Option E is incorrect as a pyogenic granuloma is a vascular lesion that bleeds easily and grows rapidly.

options.E: Pyogenic granuloma

questions.question: During the internal examination of a 45-year-old male suspected of ingesting a "bitter almond" scented substance, which safety precaution is most critical for the prosector to prevent inhalation toxicity?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the ingestion of cyanide salts reacts with gastric hydrochloric acid to release highly toxic Hydrogen Cyanide (HCN) gas; opening the stomach under a hood or running water minimizes the prosector's inhalation risk. Option A is incorrect as electric saws increase aerosolization, which is dangerous in chemical or biological exposures. Option C is incorrect as formalin is a respiratory irritant and does not neutralize cyanide gas. Option D is incorrect as standard latex gloves do not protect against gas inhalation. Option E is incorrect as lack of ventilation would lead to a lethal accumulation of toxic fumes.

options.A: Using a high-speed electric bone saw
options.B: Opening the stomach under a running water tap or fume hood
options.C: Increasing the concentration of formalin in the room
options.D: Wearing double latex gloves without a mask
options.E: Performing the autopsy in a sealed, non-ventilated room

questions.question: A prosector is preparing to remove the heart from a deceased individual with a history of cardiac conduction defects. Which physical hazard must be specifically identified and neutralized before the body is sent for cremation?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because implanted pacemakers contain batteries that can explode violently when subjected to high temperatures in a crematorium, posing a physical hazard to the facility and staff. Option A is incorrect as metallic plates do not explode, though they remain after cremation. Option B is incorrect as silicone implants melt but do not pose an explosive hazard. Option D is incorrect as intramedullary nails are inert stainless steel or titanium rods. Option E is incorrect as dental crowns are heat-resistant and non-explosive.

options.E: Porcelain dental crowns

questions.question: A forensic resident performs an autopsy on a 19-year-old student who died of a rapid-onset febrile illness with a petechial rash. Two days later, the resident is informed the blood cultures grew Neisseria meningitidis. What is the most appropriate immediate action for the resident?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because chemoprophylaxis (typically with Ciprofloxacin, Rifampin, or Ceftriaxone) is indicated for healthcare workers with high-risk exposure to respiratory secretions of a patient with meningococcal disease. Option A is incorrect as Penicillin is used for treatment, not typically for short-term prophylaxis. Option B is incorrect as vaccines do not provide immediate protection post-exposure. Option D is incorrect as these are screening tools for Tuberculosis, not Meningitis. Option E is incorrect as waiting for symptoms is dangerous given the high mortality rate of the infection.

options.A: Start a 10-day course of Penicillin G
options.C: Take a single dose of Ciprofloxacin 500mg
options.D: Undergo a chest X-ray and sputum culture
options.E: Monitor temperature for 14 days without medication

questions.question: Which chemical hazard in the mortuary is primarily associated with causing occupational asthma and severe allergic rhinitis in sensitized staff?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because glutaraldehyde, used as a disinfectant for endoscopes and sometimes in the mortuary, is a potent sensitizer known to cause occupational asthma, dermatitis, and rhinitis. Option A is incorrect as sodium hypochlorite is an irritant but less commonly a primary respiratory sensitizer than aldehydes. Option C is incorrect as ethyl alcohol is a mild irritant but not a common cause of occupational asthma. Option D is incorrect as potassium permanganate is an oxidizer and skin irritant, not a volatile respiratory sensitizer. Option E is incorrect as magnesium sulfate is a non-volatile salt used as a purgative or for edema reduction.

options.E: Magnesium Sulfate

questions.question: Following a deep scalpel cut during an autopsy on a known HIV-positive cadaver, what is the recommended timeframe for the prosector to initiate Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP) for maximum efficacy?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because PEP is most effective when initiated as soon as possible, ideally within 1-2 hours of exposure; efficacy decreases significantly as time passes. Option B is incorrect as while still offered, the efficacy is lower than immediate initiation. Option C is incorrect as 48 hours is approaching the limit of effectiveness. Option D is incorrect as 72 hours is generally considered the absolute cutoff for starting PEP. Option E is incorrect as starting after a week is considered ineffective for preventing seroconversion.

options.E: Anytime within the first week

questions.question: What is the most significant ergonomic hazard associated with the routine handling of bodies in a mortuary with limited mechanical assistance?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the manual lifting and shifting of heavy cadavers, often in awkward positions, places extreme stress on the lower back, leading to acute or chronic spinal injuries. Option A is incorrect as repetitive wrist strain is more common in fine dissection but less "significant" than spinal injury. Option C is incorrect as cervical issues are less common than lumbar issues in heavy lifting. Option D is incorrect as while standing causes foot pain, it is not the primary ergonomic hazard of body handling. Option E is incorrect as carpal tunnel is associated with vibrating tools or repetitive flexion, not heavy lifting.

options.A: Repetitive strain injury of the wrist
options.E: Carpal tunnel syndrome

questions.question: A prosector is examining a victim of suspected "Tik-20" (Diazinon) poisoning. During the procedure, the prosector develops sudden miosis, excessive salivation, and muscle fasciculations. What is the most likely route of this accidental exposure?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because organophosphorus compounds like Diazinon are highly lipid-soluble and can be absorbed through the prosector's intact skin if gloves are breached or skin is exposed to contaminated clothing/fluids. Option A is incorrect as OP compounds have low volatility; the smell is usually the solvent (kerosene), not the toxin itself. Option B is incorrect as ingestion is highly unlikely in a professional autopsy setting. Option D is incorrect as while possible, skin absorption over a larger surface area is a more common route for systemic toxicity in prosectors. Option E is incorrect as self-injection is not a standard hazard of OP autopsy.

options.E: Accidental self-injection

questions.question: During the removal of the chest plate, which physical hazard is most likely to cause a puncture wound to the prosector’s hands?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because cut or fractured ribs create extremely sharp, needle-like bone fragments that can easily penetrate latex gloves and skin during manual retraction of the chest wall. Option A is incorrect as the handle is designed for grip and is not sharp. Option B is incorrect as the sternal surface is generally smooth. Option D is incorrect as cartilage is soft and does not form sharp shards. Option E is incorrect as the blunt end of a scalpel is not a puncture hazard.

options.A: The handle of the rib shears
options.B: The smooth surface of the sternum
options.C: Sharp edges of fractured or cut ribs
options.E: The blunt end of the scalpel

questions.question: What is the approximate risk of Hepatitis C (HCV) transmission to a prosector following a single accidental percutaneous injury?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the average risk of seroconversion after a needle-stick injury from an HCV-positive source is approximately 1.8% (range 1-3%). Option A is incorrect as 0.1% is too low for HCV. Option C is incorrect as 5% is an overestimation for a single needle-stick. Option D is incorrect as 15% is significantly higher than established clinical data. Option E is incorrect as 30% is the risk associated with Hepatitis B (HBeAg positive).

questions.question: When performing an autopsy on a suspected case of Viral Hemorrhagic Fever (e.g., Ebola or Crimean-Congo), which modification to standard PPE is mandatory to mitigate the high risk of aerosol and contact transmission?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because Viral Hemorrhagic Fevers require high-level biocontainment (BSL-4) precautions, including respiratory protection (N95 or PAPR) to prevent infection from small-particle aerosols generated during the procedure. Option A is incorrect as surgical masks do not provide adequate filtration for high-risk aerosols. Option B is incorrect as double-gloving is the minimum standard for even low-risk autopsies. Option D is incorrect as cotton is permeable to fluids; fluid-resistant suits are required. Option E is incorrect as full arm protection is essential to prevent skin contact with highly infectious blood.

options.A: Use of a standard surgical mask with a face shield
options.B: Wearing a single pair of heavy-duty rubber gloves
options.C: Use of a powered air-purifying respirator (PAPR) or N95 mask
options.D: Wearing a cotton laboratory coat over scrubs
options.E: Using a plastic apron without sleeve covers

questions.question: After the en masse removal of the organ mass during a Letulle autopsy, how is the mass typically positioned on the dissection table for the initial examination of the major vessels?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because in the Letulle technique, the removed en masse organ mass is placed on the table with the anterior surface down (prone). This allows the pathologist to begin the dissection from the posterior aspect, facilitating the examination of the aorta, esophagus, and the posterior attachments of the organs. Option A is incorrect as placing it supine would obscure the posterior structures like the aorta. Option B is incorrect as vertical suspension is not a standard dissection position. Option D is incorrect because the hallmark of Letulle is the preservation of connections, so immediate separation is avoided. Option E is incorrect as autopsies are generally performed on fresh tissue to avoid fixation artifacts before gross examination.

options.E: Placed in a container of formalin for 24 hours

questions.question: What is the most significant disadvantage of the Virchow technique?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the Virchow technique involves removing organs one by one, which inherently destroys the topographical and functional connections between adjacent organ systems. Option B is incorrect because Virchow is actually one of the fastest and simplest methods. Option C is incorrect as it uses standard autopsy tools. Option D is incorrect because it is easily performed by a single person. Option E is incorrect as it is the most common technique used in adult forensic autopsies.

options.B: It is the most time-consuming method to perform
options.C: It requires highly specialized surgical instruments
options.D: It cannot be performed by a single pathologist
options.E: It is only applicable to pediatric autopsies

questions.question: In the Ghon technique, the organs are removed in functional blocks. Which of the following structures are typically included in the "pelvic block" of a male cadaver?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because the Ghon technique's pelvic block consists of the urogenital system and the distal gastrointestinal tract, specifically the bladder, prostate, and rectum in males. Option A is incorrect as kidneys and adrenals are usually part of the retroperitoneal or abdominal block. Option B is incorrect as the small intestine is part of the celiac/abdominal block. Option C is incorrect as the testes are often examined separately and are not part of the internal pelvic block. Option E is incorrect as the great vessels are usually examined with the retroperitoneal structures.

options.B: Sigmoid colon and the entire small intestine
options.D: Rectum, urinary bladder, and prostate gland
options.E: Abdominal aorta and inferior vena cava

questions.question: A 55-year-old patient dies from a suspected mesenteric artery embolism. Which autopsy technique is most superior for demonstrating the continuity between the aorta and its major visceral branches?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because the Letulle technique removes all organs en masse, preserving the entire aorta and its attachments to the mesenteric, renal, and celiac arteries, which is vital for tracing an embolism. Option A is incorrect as Virchow separates organs individually, severing these connections. Option B is incorrect as Ghon may separate the aorta from the visceral organs depending on where the blocks are divided. Option C is incorrect as Rokitansky is an in-situ dissection which makes tracing deep vascular origins difficult. Option D is incorrect as Zenker is not a standard primary autopsy technique for vascular tracing.

options.E: Letulle

questions.question: During the autopsy of a patient known to have been infected with a highly contagious hemorrhagic fever, the pathologist opts for the Rokitansky technique. What is the primary safety rationale for this choice?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the Rokitansky technique involves in-situ dissection where organs are not removed from the body. This minimizes the handling of infected tissues and reduces the risk of accidental splashes or aerosolization of infectious fluids. Option A is incorrect as in-situ dissection can be technically more difficult and slower. Option C is incorrect as the same sharp instruments are required for dissection. Option D is incorrect as a primary incision is still needed to access the cavities. Option E is incorrect as fixation is still performed after the autopsy on sampled tissues.

options.A: It allows for the fastest possible completion of the procedure
options.B: It minimizes the handling, mobilization, and transport of organs
options.C: It requires the use of fewer sharp instruments than other methods
options.D: It prevents the need for a standard primary skin incision
options.E: It facilitates the immediate fixation of organs within the body cavities

questions.question: When performing an autopsy using the Letulle technique, which structure is typically dissected first to begin the en masse removal of the organ stack?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the Letulle (en masse) technique traditionally begins at the head/neck, removing the tongue, pharynx, and larynx, and then stripping them downward along with the thoracic and abdominal viscera. Option B is incorrect as the diaphragm is usually incised later to allow the thoracic organs to pass into the abdomen. Option C is incorrect as the pelvic dissection is the final stage of the mobilization. Option D is incorrect as these are part of the mid-dissection mobilization. Option E is incorrect as the aorta is removed as part of the mass, not dissected first.

options.B: The diaphragm at its costal attachments
options.E: The abdominal aorta at the bifurcation

questions.question: A pathologist is performing a Ghon autopsy. Which of the following groups of organs constitutes the "coeliac block" (also known as the second block)?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because the Ghon technique divides organs into functional blocks; the second or "coeliac" block typically includes the liver, spleen, pancreas, stomach, and duodenum, preserving the biliary and pancreatic duct connections. Option A is incorrect as these form the thoracic (first) block. Option B is incorrect as these form the retroperitoneal block. Option C is incorrect as these form the pelvic block. Option D is incorrect as the small intestine (jejunum/ileum) is often handled separately from the coeliac block.

options.C: Urinary bladder, rectum, and internal genitalia
options.D: Lower esophagus, stomach, and small intestines
options.E: Liver, spleen, pancreas, stomach, and duodenum

questions.question: A pathologist needs to examine the thoracic duct for suspected chylothorax. Why is the Virchow technique considered the least effective for this purpose?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the thoracic duct is a fragile structure that runs along the spine; in the Virchow technique, removing the lungs and heart individually almost always severs or destroys the duct. Option A is incorrect as time is not the primary limiting factor compared to structural integrity. Option C is incorrect as the brain has no relation to the thoracic duct. Option D is incorrect as the limitation is macroscopic dissection, not staining. Option E is incorrect as the duct is present in all humans; Letulle simply preserves it better.

options.A: It requires too much time to locate the duct
options.B: The duct is easily damaged or lost when organs are removed individually
options.C: The duct cannot be visualized without removing the brain first
options.D: It requires specialized chemical stains not used in the Virchow method
options.E: The duct is only anatomically present in the Letulle technique

questions.question: A cadaver is found to have complete situs inversus totalis. Which autopsy technique would be most effective in documenting the reversed anatomical orientation of all organ systems simultaneously?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because the Letulle technique removes all organs as a single mass, allowing the pathologist to demonstrate the reversed relationship of the heart, lungs, liver, and spleen in one continuous unit. Option A is incorrect as it separates the organs, losing the "total" picture. Option B is incorrect as it breaks the body into blocks, which may obscure the overall orientation. Option C is incorrect as in-situ dissection (Rokitansky) makes it harder to photograph or demonstrate the mass as a whole outside the body. Option E is incorrect as Griesinger is not a standard technique for this purpose.

options.E: Griesinger

questions.question: During the "en masse" removal in the Letulle technique, which plane of dissection is utilized to ensure the aorta and esophagus remain attached to the organ mass?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the pathologist dissects along the pre-vertebral fascia, stripping the soft tissues (including the aorta and esophagus) away from the vertebral column to keep them with the organ mass. Option A is incorrect as dissecting the retro-esophageal space would separate the esophagus from the aorta. Option B is incorrect as the ligament is part of the spine and is left behind. Option D is incorrect as the endothoracic fascia is more lateral. Option E is incorrect as it is too superficial to ensure the vessels remain with the mass.

options.E: The para-aortic soft tissue

questions.question: A patient dies three days after a total gastrectomy with esophagojejunostomy due to suspected anastomotic leakage. Which autopsy technique is most appropriate to preserve the integrity of the surgical site?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the Letulle technique removes the esophagus, stomach (or surgical site), and intestines as one unit, ensuring the anastomosis is not stressed or severed during removal. Option A is incorrect as it would involve cutting through the anastomosis to remove organs one by one. Option C is incorrect as the Ghon technique might divide the block at the level of the anastomosis. Option D is incorrect as in-situ dissection provides poor visibility for testing the integrity of a leak (e.g., by water testing). Option E is incorrect as Virchow is fundamentally unsuitable for preserving connections.

options.E: Modified Virchow

questions.question: Which autopsy technique, developed in Prague, is characterized by the removal of organs in functional blocks to balance anatomical preservation with ease of handling?
questions.explanation: Option E is correct because the Ghon technique was developed by Anton Ghon in Prague and is defined by the "en bloc" removal of organ systems. Option A is incorrect as Letulle is the French "en masse" method. Option B is incorrect as Virchow is the German "organ-by-organ" method. Option C is incorrect as Rokitansky is the Austrian "in-situ" method. Option D is incorrect as Zenker is a variation not typically associated with the primary Prague school of blocks.

options.E: Ghon

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: What is the standard weight of the liver that must be preserved for toxicological analysis during a forensic autopsy?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because 500 grams is the standard recommended quantity of the liver to be preserved for chemical analysis to ensure a sufficient sample for multiple toxicological tests. Option A is incorrect as 100 grams is insufficient for a comprehensive toxicological screen. Option B is incorrect as it is half the required standard amount. Option D is incorrect as 750 grams exceeds the standard requirement for routine cases. Option E is incorrect as preserving the entire liver (approx. 1500g) is unnecessary and creates storage issues.

options.E: 1000 grams

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: A medical officer is performing an autopsy and needs to preserve a portion of the heart for histopathological examination to rule out myocardial infarction. Which preservative should be used?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because 10% Formalin is the standard fixative used for histopathological analysis as it preserves cellular architecture and prevents autolysis. Option A is incorrect because rectified spirit is used for toxicological preservation but hardens tissues and distorts cellular details for microscopy. Option B is incorrect because saturated sodium chloride is used for toxicology in cases of corrosive poisoning, not for histopathology. Option D is incorrect because magnesium sulphate is not a tissue preservative. Option E is incorrect because absolute alcohol causes excessive shrinkage of tissues, making histopathological interpretation difficult.

options.E: Absolute alcohol

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: When collecting a blood sample for the estimation of ethyl alcohol, which combination of chemicals must be added to the container?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because sodium fluoride acts as a preservative to prevent the glycolysis and neo-formation of alcohol by microorganisms, while potassium oxalate acts as an anticoagulant. Option A is incorrect because while EDTA is an anticoagulant, it does not prevent the post-mortem fermentation of alcohol. Option C is incorrect because sodium chloride is not used as a blood preservative for alcohol. Option D is incorrect because formalin interferes with the chemical detection of many poisons and is not used for blood samples. Option E is incorrect because potassium permanganate is an oxidizing agent and would destroy the alcohol in the sample.

options.E: Potassium permanganate and Acetic acid

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: A 30-year-old man is suspected to have died from an intravenous injection of a lethal narcotic. During the autopsy, what is the correct protocol for collecting the primary evidence from the injection site?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the standard protocol for suspected injection deaths requires excising the affected area (skin and subcutaneous tissue) to detect unabsorbed poison, along with a control sample from a non-injected site for comparison. Option A is incorrect because swabbing the surface will not recover the injected material and spirit may interfere with analysis. Option B is incorrect because aspiration is insufficient to recover the drug fixed in the tissues. Option D is incorrect because the skin and subcutaneous tissues are the primary sites for detecting the depot of the drug. Option E is incorrect because while blood is needed, the localized tissue at the injection site is the most critical evidence for proving the route of administration.

options.A: Swab the skin surface with rectified spirit and preserve the swab.
options.B: Aspirate the underlying tissue fluid using a sterile syringe.
options.C: Excise the skin and subcutaneous tissue of the site along with a control sample from the opposite limb.
options.D: Preserve only the underlying vein in a saturated salt solution.
options.E: Collect 100ml of blood specifically from the vein proximal to the puncture.

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: A medical officer has completed a forensic autopsy in a high-profile poisoning case. To ensure the "Chain of Custody" and prevent legal challenges regarding tampering, what is the mandatory protocol for dispatching the seals to the Forensic Science Laboratory (FSL)?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the dispatch protocol requires that a sample of the seal used on the viscera jars must be sent separately to the FSL so the analyst can verify that the jars arrived untampered. Option A is incorrect because placing a seal inside the jar is not a standard protocol and does not prevent external tampering. Option B is incorrect because the original seal (the physical die) is never sent; only the impression is sent. Option D is incorrect because every individual container must be sealed to ensure the integrity of each sample. Option E is incorrect because the FSL cannot begin analysis without a verified seal impression for comparison.

options.A: Place a duplicate seal inside the viscera jar before closing it.
options.B: Hand over the viscera jars and the original seal to the police constable.
options.C: Send a copy of the seal used on the jars in a separate sealed envelope along with the requisition form.
options.D: Affix the seal only on the outer wooden box and not on the individual jars.
options.E: Keep the seal in the hospital locker until the chemical examiner requests it.

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: In a suspected case of poisoning by vegetable alkaloids such as Aconite, the medical officer decides to preserve the brain. To prevent the chemical degradation of these specific alkaloids, what modification is required in the storage medium?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because alkaloids are more stable in an acidic medium; adding acetic acid to rectified spirit prevents their hydrolysis and degradation during storage. Option A is incorrect because formalin is used for histopathology and can chemically react with certain alkaloids, making their detection difficult. Option C is incorrect because sodium bicarbonate creates an alkaline medium, which promotes the decomposition of many alkaloids. Option D is incorrect because without a preservative, the brain will undergo rapid autolysis and putrefaction. Option E is incorrect because potassium permanganate is an oxidizer that would destroy the alkaloids.

options.B: Add a small amount of acetic acid to the rectified spirit.
options.C: Use saturated sodium chloride with sodium bicarbonate.
options.D: Preserve the brain in a dry, airtight container without any liquid.
options.E: Use absolute alcohol mixed with potassium permanganate.

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: During a forensic autopsy, how much of the stomach and its contents should be preserved for toxicological analysis?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the entire stomach and all its contents must be preserved to ensure that the total amount of unabsorbed poison can be calculated and to examine the mucosa for local effects. Option A is incorrect because the stomach wall itself may contain absorbed or adherent poison. Option B is incorrect because a small piece is insufficient for a full toxicological screen. Option D and E are incorrect because preserving only a portion of the stomach may miss localized areas of corrosion or concentrated poison.

options.A: 100 ml of the gastric contents only.
options.B: A small piece of the stomach wall from the greater curvature.
options.C: The whole stomach along with all its contents.
options.D: The cardiac end of the stomach with 50 ml of contents.
options.E: The pyloric end of the stomach only.

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: A pathologist is performing an autopsy on a victim suspected of acute cyanide poisoning. Which precaution is most critical for the safety of the autopsy team during the collection of viscera?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because hydrocyanic acid gas can be released upon opening the stomach or body cavities, posing a lethal inhalation risk to the pathologist and assistants. Option A is incorrect because standard masks do not filter out cyanide gas. Option B is incorrect because metal instruments do not react with cyanide in a way that poses an immediate safety risk. Option D is incorrect because glass jars are preferred for toxicology to avoid plasticizer interference. Option E is incorrect because neutralizing the contents would destroy the poison, preventing its detection by the chemical examiner.

options.A: Wearing double masks to avoid the smell of bitter almonds.
options.B: Avoiding the use of any metal instruments during the dissection.
options.C: Ensuring the room is well-ventilated and avoiding direct inhalation of fumes when opening the stomach.
options.D: Using only plastic jars for the preservation of the stomach.
options.E: Neutralizing the stomach contents with an alkali before collection.

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: In a case of advanced putrefaction where the internal organs are liquefied and routine viscera cannot be collected, which sample is most reliable for detecting chronic metallic poisoning?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because metallic poisons like lead, arsenic, and antimony are deposited in hard tissues like bones and teeth, which resist putrefaction for years. Option A and B are incorrect because liquefied tissues and fluids are highly contaminated by putrefactive by-products and may lose the poison. Option D is incorrect because adipocere primarily contains fatty acid derivatives and is not the primary site for metallic deposition. Option E is incorrect because muscle tissue undergoes rapid decomposition and is less reliable than bone in advanced putrefaction.

options.E: Muscle from the gluteal region

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: According to the standard autopsy protocol, which of the following must be clearly mentioned on the label of every viscera jar?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the name of the deceased and the police case number (FIR/MLC number) are essential for identification and maintaining the chain of custody. Option A is incorrect because the suspected poison is mentioned in the requisition form, not necessarily on the jar label. Option B is incorrect because the next of kin's name is not a requirement for forensic labeling. Option D is incorrect because the clinical history is provided in the autopsy report and requisition, not the label. Option E is incorrect because the weight of the empty jar is not a standard labeling requirement.

options.A: The suspected name of the poison.
options.B: The name of the next of kin.
options.C: The name of the deceased and the police station case number.
options.D: The clinical history of the patient.
options.E: The weight of the empty jar.

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: What is the minimum quantity of the kidney that should be preserved for toxicological analysis in a routine forensic autopsy?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because the standard protocol requires preserving half of each kidney to provide a representative sample of the renal tissue for analysis. Option A is incorrect because preserving only one kidney may miss pathology or poison distribution present in the other. Option C and D are incorrect because both the cortex and medulla are needed as different poisons concentrate in different parts of the nephron. Option E is incorrect because 10 grams is an insufficient quantity for a full toxicological analysis.

options.C: Only the cortex of one kidney
options.D: Only the medulla of both kidneys
options.E: 10 grams of tissue from the upper pole

questions.lo: For1-Au-006. Collection of viscera at autopsy
questions.question: A body is recovered from a canal in a highly decomposed state. The medical officer is unable to obtain a viable blood sample for alcohol analysis. Which alternative biological fluid should be prioritized for collection as it is well-protected and correlates well with blood alcohol levels?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the vitreous humor is anatomically sequestered and protected from putrefactive changes, making it an excellent alternative for alcohol and electrolyte estimation in decomposed bodies. Option A is incorrect because bile is prone to contamination and chemical changes during putrefaction. Option B is incorrect because pericardial fluid is often contaminated by diffusion from the stomach or lungs after death. Option D is incorrect because gastric washings only show unabsorbed alcohol, not the systemic concentration. Option E is incorrect because synovial fluid is not routinely used and is harder to collect in sufficient quantities.

options.E: Synovial fluid

questions.question: What is the mandatory requirement for all windows in a standard autopsy suite?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because fly-proof mesh screens are essential to prevent insects from entering the suite, which could otherwise contaminate bodies or act as vectors for pathogens. Option A is incorrect as double-glazing is primarily for thermal insulation and is not a specific forensic requirement. Option B is incorrect as UV protection is not a standard requirement for autopsy windows. Option C is incorrect because windows may be designed to open for emergency ventilation, provided they are properly screened. Option E is incorrect as bullet-proofing is only necessary in high-security specialized facilities, not standard suites.

options.E: Bullet-proof reinforcement

questions.question: In a standard autopsy suite, what is the primary purpose of the anteroom located between the clean and dirty zones?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because an anteroom serves as a transition space where staff can safely put on (don) and take off (doff) protective gear to prevent cross-contamination between zones. Option A is incorrect as equipment storage should be in a dedicated tool room to maintain the anteroom's hygiene. Option B is incorrect as bodies are stored in the mortuary refrigerators, not in transition rooms. Option D is incorrect as all examinations should occur on the autopsy table within the dirty zone. Option E is incorrect as HVAC controls are typically located in a separate technical or maintenance area.

options.C: Donning and doffing of personal protective equipment
options.E: Housing the central HVAC control system

questions.question: A forensic pathologist is performing an autopsy on a suspected case of multidrug-resistant tuberculosis. Which specific environmental control is required for the isolation room used in this case?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because HEPA filtration of exhaust air is mandatory for isolation rooms to ensure that infectious particles like TB bacilli are removed before the air is vented outside. Option A is incorrect because isolation rooms must have negative pressure to prevent contaminated air from flowing into clean corridors. Option B is incorrect as air from infectious cases must not be recirculated; it must be exhausted. Option D is incorrect as natural ventilation is uncontrolled and risks spreading pathogens to the environment. Option E is incorrect as high humidity can actually promote the survival of certain microbes and does not replace filtration.

options.A: Positive air pressure relative to the corridor
options.B: Recirculation of air through standard filters
options.C: High-Efficiency Particulate Air (HEPA) filtration of exhaust
options.E: Increased humidity levels to settle airborne droplets

questions.question: During the renovation of a forensic facility, the architect suggests adding sound-absorbing panels to the autopsy room. What is the primary functional reason for this requirement?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because autopsy rooms are often constructed with hard, non-porous surfaces that cause significant echo; sound-absorbing panels are necessary to ensure that dictated findings are clear and accurate. Option A is incorrect as external noise insulation is a separate structural requirement, not the primary reason for internal panels. Option C is incorrect because while noise reduction helps, the primary forensic requirement is the accuracy of the record. Option D is incorrect as industrial codes are general and do not address the specific dictation needs of a forensic suite. Option E is incorrect as viewing galleries have their own acoustic requirements separate from the main work floor.

options.A: To prevent the noise of bone saws from reaching the public
options.B: To improve the clarity of voice-recorded autopsy findings
options.C: To reduce the psychological stress on the autopsy technicians
options.D: To comply with local building codes for industrial workspaces
options.E: To minimize the echo during student viewing sessions

questions.question: A forensic department is implementing a new waste management protocol. Which of the following is the correct disposal method for the disposable plastic aprons used during a routine autopsy?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because soft, non-sharp infectious waste like plastic aprons must be placed in yellow biohazard bags for high-temperature incineration. Option B is incorrect as red bags are typically used for heavy plastic or items requiring autoclaving before disposal, but yellow is the standard for infectious clinical waste. Option C is incorrect as blue containers are reserved for sharps like needles and scalpel blades. Option D is incorrect as contaminated PPE can never be treated as general municipal waste. Option E is incorrect as green bags are usually reserved for biodegradable or non-clinical waste depending on local hospital protocols.

options.B: Red bags for autoclaving and landfill
options.D: General black waste bins for municipal collection
options.E: Green bags for chemical decontamination

questions.question: What is the standard requirement for the water supply at the autopsy table?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because a mix of hot and cold water, controlled by a mixing valve, is necessary for effective cleaning of the body and the table surfaces. Option A is incorrect as cold water alone is ineffective at removing fatty tissues or congealed blood. Option B is incorrect as distilled water is unnecessary and too expensive for routine cleaning. Option C is incorrect as steam is dangerous to the operator and can damage tissues during the procedure. Option E is incorrect as deionized water is used in laboratories, not for general autopsy table irrigation.

options.A: Cold water only to prevent bacterial growth
options.B: Distilled water for all rinsing procedures
options.D: Both hot and cold water with a mixing valve
options.E: Deionized water to prevent mineral staining

questions.question: In the administrative "Clean Zone" of a forensic facility, which feature is essential for maintaining the integrity of the "Dirty-to-Clean" workflow?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the clean zone must have a separate ventilation system (or be under positive pressure) to prevent the migration of odors and pathogens from the autopsy room. Option A is incorrect as pass-through windows for samples should lead to the laboratory, not the administrative clean zone. Option B is incorrect as direct access would violate the clean-to-dirty separation. Option D is incorrect as the clean zone should not have drains connected to the contaminated autopsy lines to prevent backflow of odors. Option E is incorrect as chemicals should be stored in the dirty zone or a dedicated chemical store, not in the administrative area.

options.A: A dedicated pass-through window for tissue samples
options.B: Direct access to the body storage refrigerators
options.C: A separate ventilation system from the autopsy room
options.D: Floor drains connected to the main autopsy drainage
options.E: Storage cabinets for formalin and other fixatives

questions.question: A forensic facility is being audited for chemical safety. Where should the bulk stock of concentrated formalin be stored according to standard safety requirements?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because formalin is both toxic and flammable; it must be stored in a dedicated, ventilated, fire-rated cabinet to prevent the buildup of fumes and minimize fire risks. Option A is incorrect as open shelving allows fumes to escape and increases the risk of accidental spills. Option C is incorrect as cold storage is not required for formalin and would contaminate the body storage area with fumes. Option D is incorrect as administrative areas must remain chemical-free. Option E is incorrect as the anteroom is a clean transition space and not suitable for bulk chemical storage.

options.A: On the open shelves in the autopsy room for easy access
options.B: In a ventilated, fire-rated chemical storage cabinet
options.C: Inside the walk-in body refrigerator to reduce fumes
options.D: In the administrative office for better inventory control
options.E: Under the scrub-up sinks in the anteroom

questions.question: What is the recommended ergonomic height for a fixed autopsy table to accommodate the average operator?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because a height of approximately 85 cm (or between 80-90 cm) is the standard ergonomic height that allows pathologists to work comfortably without excessive bending or reaching. Option A is incorrect as 60 cm is too low and would cause significant back strain. Option C is incorrect as 110 cm is too high for the average person to perform dissections effectively. Options D and E are incorrect as these heights are near chest or shoulder level, making work impossible.

options.E: 150 cm

questions.question: To ensure the security of a forensic facility, what is the standard requirement for the "Body Release" area?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because the release of a body to funeral directors should be done through a private, secure exit to maintain the dignity of the deceased and prevent public distress. Option A is incorrect as separating intake and release helps manage workflow and security. Option B is incorrect as the transfer of bodies should never be visible to the public. Option D is incorrect as the release area is part of the dirty/transitional zone and should not be in the clean administrative area. Option E is incorrect as the viewing gallery is for observation, not for body transport.

options.A: It must be the same as the "Body Intake" entrance
options.B: It must be visible from the public waiting room
options.C: It must have a separate, secure exit away from public view
options.D: It must be located within the administrative clean zone
options.E: It must be accessible only through the viewing gallery

questions.question: During a high-profile forensic investigation, the lead pathologist requires a "Double-Block" security protocol. Which architectural feature supports this requirement?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because "Double-Block" or "Dual-Control" security refers to evidence management where two different authorized individuals are required to access high-priority evidence. Option A is incorrect as multiple tables relate to capacity, not security. Option C is incorrect as this is a standard layout for odor control, not a specific security protocol. Option D is incorrect as this is a utility requirement for power failure. Option E is incorrect as this relates to plumbing and fluid management.

options.A: Two separate autopsy tables in one room
options.B: A dual-key system for the evidence storage locker
options.C: Two sets of doors between the mortuary and the public
options.D: A backup generator for the refrigeration unit
options.E: Two separate drainage systems for the autopsy floor

questions.question: Which of the following is the most appropriate disinfectant for the daily cleaning of stainless steel autopsy tables?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because sodium hypochlorite (bleach) is the standard, effective, and economical disinfectant used to kill a wide range of pathogens on autopsy surfaces. Option A is incorrect as nitric acid is a corrosive poison and would damage the stainless steel. Option B is incorrect as alcohol is a good antiseptic for skin but is less effective than bleach for large-scale surface disinfection in a high-load environment. Option D is incorrect as boiling water is impractical for large surfaces and does not provide chemical disinfection. Option E is incorrect as soap is a cleanser but not a high-level disinfectant.

options.E: Liquid hand soap

questions.question: Which of the following is categorized as a psychological hazard for a forensic pathologist?
questions.explanation: Option B is correct because repeated exposure to mutilated bodies, child autopsies, and gruesome crime scenes can lead to significant psychological stress and post-traumatic stress disorder. Option A is a chemical hazard. Option C is a biological/physical hazard. Option D is a physical hazard related to radiation. Option E is a physical hazard related to the environment.

options.E: Slippery floors

questions.question: A pathologist is performing an autopsy on a patient who recently underwent brachytherapy for prostate cancer. What is the primary hazard in this case?
questions.explanation: Option C is correct because brachytherapy involves the implantation of radioactive seeds, which pose a risk of ionizing radiation to the prosector during organ removal. Option A is a chemical hazard present in most autopsies but not specific to cancer therapy implants. Option B is a risk associated with faulty electrical equipment. Option D is a risk associated with infectious diseases. Option E is a physical hazard from fractured bones.

options.E: Sharp bone fragments

questions.question: A 20-year-old student died within 24 hours of developing a high-grade fever, neck stiffness, and a petechial rash. During the autopsy, which precaution is most critical to prevent the primary biological hazard associated with this specific case?
questions.explanation: Option A is correct because the clinical history suggests Meningococcal meningitis (Neisseria meningitidis), which requires post-exposure chemoprophylaxis (like Ciprofloxacin or Rifampicin) for those exposed to respiratory secretions or aerosols during the examination. Option B is for radiation hazards. Option C is to prevent aerosolization of bone dust, primarily for TB or CJD. Option D is a general measure for electrical safety. Option E is a general ventilation requirement but not the specific clinical intervention for Meningococcus.

options.D: Avoiding the use of water during the procedure
options.E: Ensuring the room has 20 air changes per hour

questions.question: During the autopsy of a victim found in a tannery vat, the prosector notices a strong smell of rotten eggs. What is the primary hazard?
questions.explanation: Option D is correct because hydrogen sulphide (H2S) is commonly found in sewers and tanneries, characterized by a rotten egg smell and the ability to cause rapid respiratory paralysis in the prosector if not properly ventilated. Option A is an odorless gas. Option B is an explosive gas but not characterized by this smell. Option C is an odorless asphyxiant. Option E has a pungent, suffocating smell but is not the primary gas associated with decaying organic matter in tanneries.

options.E: Sulfur dioxide
